topic: Traits - Index Trait for Custom Indexing

turns.content: Imagine we are building a tool to automate a repetitive task where every step is represented by a unique state ID stored in our newtype wrapper. I want the system to allow direct access like steps[0] and steps[1], so I'm planning to implement Index<usize> on my struct.

That is a great goal for making your tool feel native, but be careful not to confuse Index with the iterator protocol. Implementing Index<usize> tells the compiler how to get an element at position n and return it directly, whereas Iterator provides a chain of yield expressions that builds a sequence lazily. For direct indexing in your automation context, you need the Index trait specifically.
Got it, so I just add an index method? But what if I also want to set values later without needing to find them first?

Exactly. To support setting values efficiently, you will need to implement the IndexMut trait alongside Index. Think of it like this: Index is a read-only door that lets you peek inside the array, while IndexMut is an unlocked window that lets you change what's stored at any position without searching through the whole collection first.
That analogy helps. I see now that having both traits means my wrapper can behave like a slice, which is crucial for performance in our tool since we don't want to iterate every time.

Precisely. By implementing Index and IndexMut on your newtype, you grant it the same low-level access patterns that native arrays provide, which is exactly what you need for efficient lookups and updates in an automated workflow.
